inline_review:
commit 5519152dd8ec28f6a05a8c4d002224d4a6a140e3
Author: Aleksandr Nogikh <nogikh@google.com>

x86/kexec: Disable KCOV instrumentation after load_segments()

This commit disables KCOV instrumentation for machine_kexec_64.o and
physaddr.o to prevent crashes during kexec. The crashes occur because
load_segments() invalidates the GS base, which KCOV uses to access
per-CPU data, leading to a crash loop in subsequent instrumented code.

> diff --git a/arch/x86/kernel/Makefile b/arch/x86/kernel/Makefile
> index e9aeeeafad17..febf6f49207b 100644
> --- a/arch/x86/kernel/Makefile
> +++ b/arch/x86/kernel/Makefile
[ ... ]
> @@ -43,6 +43,17 @@ KCOV_INSTRUMENT_dumpstack_$(BITS).o			:= n
>  KCOV_INSTRUMENT_unwind_orc.o				:= n
>  KCOV_INSTRUMENT_unwind_frame.o				:= n
>  KCOV_INSTRUMENT_unwind_guess.o				:= n
> +# Disable KCOV to prevent crashes during kexec: load_segments() invalidates
> +# the GS base, which KCOV relies on for per-CPU data.
> +# As KCOV && KEXEC compatibility should be preserved (e.g. syzkaller is
> +# using it to collect crash dumps during kernel fuzzing), disabling
> +# KCOV for KEXEC kernels is not an option. Selectively disabling KCOV
> +# instrumentation for individual affected functions can be fragile, while
> +# adding more checks to KCOV would slow it down.
> +# As a compromise solution, disable KCOV instrumentation for the whole
> +# source code file. If its coverage is ever needed, other approaches
> +# should be considered.
> +KCOV_INSTRUMENT_machine_kexec_64.o			:= n

Is the CONFIG_KEXEC_JUMP path still affected by the crash loop this tries to
fix?

When relocate_kernel() returns, execution resumes in machine_kexec():

	/* now call it */
	image->start = relocate_kernel_ptr(...);

#ifdef CONFIG_KEXEC_JUMP
	if (image->preserve_context)
		restore_processor_state();
#endif

Since restore_processor_state() is located in arch/x86/power/cpu.c, and that
file is not excluded from KCOV instrumentation, would the compiler insert a
call to __sanitizer_cov_trace_pc() at the start of the function?

If so, this would trigger per-CPU accesses with a zeroed GS base before the
function has a chance to restore MSR_GS_BASE, leading to the exact same crash
loop.

Would it be better to address this architecturally, such as restoring
MSR_GS_BASE natively in the assembly return path of relocate_kernel()?

> diff --git a/arch/x86/mm/Makefile b/arch/x86/mm/Makefile
> index 5b9908f13dcf..3a5364853eab 100644
> --- a/arch/x86/mm/Makefile
> +++ b/arch/x86/mm/Makefile
[ ... ]
> @@ -4,6 +4,8 @@ KCOV_INSTRUMENT_tlb.o			:= n
>  KCOV_INSTRUMENT_mem_encrypt.o		:= n
>  KCOV_INSTRUMENT_mem_encrypt_amd.o	:= n
>  KCOV_INSTRUMENT_pgprot.o		:= n
> +# See the "Disable KCOV" comment in arch/x86/kernel/Makefile.
> +KCOV_INSTRUMENT_physaddr.o		:= n

Does this patch mask an underlying ABI issue by disabling instrumentation rather
than fixing the argument evaluation order?

In machine_kexec(), several C arguments and memory accesses are evaluated
after load_segments() is called:

	load_segments();

	/* now call it */
	image->start = relocate_kernel_ptr((unsigned long)image->head,
					   virt_to_phys(control_page),
					   image->start,
					   relocate_kernel_flags);

If CONFIG_DEBUG_VIRTUAL=y is set, virt_to_phys(control_page) expands to
__phys_addr().

A comment in machine_kexec() explicitly warns: "Don't make any function call
after here since call depth tracking uses percpu variables".

Would executing __phys_addr(), or reading image->head and image->start,
cause crashes if Call Depth Tracking or sanitizers like KMSAN are enabled?

Sanitizers often intercept accesses using per-CPU metadata contexts, which
would crash while the GS base is zero.

Could we avoid the need to disable KCOV for physaddr.o entirely by
pre-evaluating these arguments into local variables before calling
load_segments()?

Since physaddr.c contains fundamental physical address translation logic used
throughout the kernel, leaving it instrumented seems valuable for fuzzing
core memory management paths.

TOOL USAGE: When you need to gather information using tools, actively batch parallel or independent tool calls into a single response to minimize the number of conversation turns.

<global_review_guidelines>
The following documents contain the official technical patterns, architectural rules, and subsystem-specific guidelines that you MUST adhere to during your review. Use these as the absolute source of truth for identifying anti-patterns and violations.

@technical-patterns.md

Target Commit:
commit 5519152dd8ec28f6a05a8c4d002224d4a6a140e3
Author: Aleksandr Nogikh <nogikh@google.com>
Date:   Wed Mar 25 15:48:24 2026 +0000

    x86/kexec: Disable KCOV instrumentation after load_segments()
    
    The load_segments() function changes segment registers, invalidating
    GS base (which KCOV relies on for per-cpu data). When CONFIG_KCOV is
    enabled, any subsequent instrumented C code call (e.g.
    native_gdt_invalidate()) begins crashing the kernel in an endless
    loop.
    
    To reproduce the problem, it's sufficient to do kexec on a
    KCOV-instrumented kernel:
    $ kexec -l /boot/otherKernel
    $ kexec -e
    
    The real-world context for this problem is enabling crash dump
    collection in syzkaller. For this, the tool loads a panic kernel
    before fuzzing and then calls makedumpfile after the panic. This
    workflow requires both CONFIG_KEXEC and CONFIG_KCOV to be enabled
    simultaneously.
    
    Adding safeguards directly to the KCOV fast-path
    (__sanitizer_cov_trace_pc()) is also undesirable as it would
    introduce an extra performance overhead.
    
    Disabling instrumentation for the individual functions would be too
    fragile, so disable KCOV instrumentation for the entire
    machine_kexec_64.c and physaddr.c. If coverage-guided fuzzing ever
    needs these components in the future, other approaches should be
    considered.
    
    The problem is not relevant for 32 bit kernels as CONFIG_KCOV is not
    supported there.
    
    Reviewed-by: Dmitry Vyukov <dvyukov@google.com>
    Signed-off-by: Aleksandr Nogikh <nogikh@google.com>
    Fixes: 0d345996e4cb ("x86/kernel: increase kcov coverage under arch/x86/kernel folder")
    Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org

diff --git a/arch/x86/kernel/Makefile b/arch/x86/kernel/Makefile
index e9aeeeafad17..febf6f49207b 100644
--- a/arch/x86/kernel/Makefile
+++ b/arch/x86/kernel/Makefile
@@ -43,6 +43,17 @@ KCOV_INSTRUMENT_dumpstack_$(BITS).o			:= n
 KCOV_INSTRUMENT_unwind_orc.o				:= n
 KCOV_INSTRUMENT_unwind_frame.o				:= n
 KCOV_INSTRUMENT_unwind_guess.o				:= n
+# Disable KCOV to prevent crashes during kexec: load_segments() invalidates
+# the GS base, which KCOV relies on for per-CPU data.
+# As KCOV && KEXEC compatibility should be preserved (e.g. syzkaller is
+# using it to collect crash dumps during kernel fuzzing), disabling
+# KCOV for KEXEC kernels is not an option. Selectively disabling KCOV
+# instrumentation for individual affected functions can be fragile, while
+# adding more checks to KCOV would slow it down.
+# As a compromise solution, disable KCOV instrumentation for the whole
+# source code file. If its coverage is ever needed, other approaches
+# should be considered.
+KCOV_INSTRUMENT_machine_kexec_64.o			:= n
 
 CFLAGS_head32.o := -fno-stack-protector
 CFLAGS_head64.o := -fno-stack-protector
diff --git a/arch/x86/mm/Makefile b/arch/x86/mm/Makefile
index 5b9908f13dcf..3a5364853eab 100644
--- a/arch/x86/mm/Makefile
+++ b/arch/x86/mm/Makefile
@@ -4,6 +4,8 @@ KCOV_INSTRUMENT_tlb.o			:= n
 KCOV_INSTRUMENT_mem_encrypt.o		:= n
 KCOV_INSTRUMENT_mem_encrypt_amd.o	:= n
 KCOV_INSTRUMENT_pgprot.o		:= n
+# See the "Disable KCOV" comment in arch/x86/kernel/Makefile.
+KCOV_INSTRUMENT_physaddr.o		:= n
 
 KASAN_SANITIZE_mem_encrypt.o		:= n
 KASAN_SANITIZE_mem_encrypt_amd.o	:= n

    "thought": "**Analyzing the KCOV Disabling Commit**

Okay, so I'm looking at this commit, and my initial thought is that it disables KCOV instrumentation for `arch/x86/kernel/machine_kexec_64.o` and `arch/x86/mm/physaddr.o`. The core reason seems to be that `load_segments()` in `machine_kexec_64.c` messes with the GS base, which KCOV relies on to access per-CPU data, leading to kernel crashes in subsequently instrumented code. The commit specifically mentions that we need to consider alternative approaches if we *ever* want to use coverage-guided fuzzing on these components in the future.

My first check is to verify how this is achieved. The commit sets `KCOV_INSTRUMENT_machine_kexec_64.o := n` and `KCOV_INSTRUMENT_physaddr.o := n`.  Okay, that makes sense. It's file-specific, and that's reflected in the `arch/x86/kernel/Makefile` and `arch/x86/mm/Makefile`, respectively. This is a common pattern in the kernel.  We use this approach to disable instrumentation for entire files when finer-grained disabling is too brittle, say due to inlining or compiler optimization. Considering the codebases, this feels like an acceptable level of granularity.  `machine_kexec_64.c` and `physaddr.c` are relatively contained, and I can see this being a reasonable compromise.

I'm thinking now about what the commit *actually* does. It looks like it directly modifies those Makefiles, setting those KCOV variables to "n". That seems correct based on the commit message and the syntax I'm familiar with in the kernel's build system.

Now, a broader perspective: Does this solve the *specific* problem? Yes, the instrumentation is disabled, so there shouldn't be any GS base-related crashes triggered by KCOV.  

But what about the bigger picture?  Is there anything else called *after* `load_segments()` that might be problematic? That's the key question. I should probably check the `machine_kexec()` function itself in `arch/x86/kernel/machine_kexec_64.c` to be completely sure.

    "thought": "**Analyzing the KCOV Disabling in machine_kexec_64.o and physaddr.o**

Okay, so this commit disables KCOV for `machine_kexec_64.o` and `physaddr.o`. Time to really dig into why, especially the `machine_kexec_64.c` part. The core of the issue seems to be the interaction between `load_segments()` and the instrumentation of code using per-CPU data when KCOV is enabled.

In `machine_kexec()`, we have `load_segments()` at the beginning. That function loads the segment registers, specifically, `%gs` is loaded with `__KERNEL_DS`. Now, that's where things get interesting.  `__KERNEL_DS` is a 32-bit segment with a base of 0.  In x86-64, the `%gs` base is usually used to access per-CPU data.  By resetting `%gs` to 0, it essentially invalidates any previous setup for accessing per-CPU data via `%gs`.

The critical problem is that `virt_to_phys()` is called AFTER `load_segments()`. This is important because `virt_to_phys()` ultimately calls `__phys_addr()`, which is in `arch/x86/mm/physaddr.c`. Now, if KCOV is enabled, both `__phys_addr()` and potentially even the inlined `virt_to_phys()` will be instrumented. This means `__sanitizer_cov_trace_pc()` gets called. And here is the kicker: `__sanitizer_cov_trace_pc()` needs to access `current` using `get_current()`. `get_current()` uses `pcpu_hot.current_task`, which, as you know, is a per-CPU variable accessed via `%gs:offset`. But `%gs` base has just been reset to zero! Therefore, any attempt to access per-CPU data will cause a #GP or page fault at an address very close to 0. It's perfectly clear now; this is why `__phys_addr()` needs to be excluded.

But wait a minute! The code after `load_segments()` still includes an indirect function call through `relocate_kernel_ptr()`. If `machine_kexec()` is KCOV-instrumented, then the code *after* `load_segments()` will have `__sanitizer_cov_trace_pc()` calls! That's why the entire file, `machine_kexec_64.c`, must be excluded.

Hold on a second, what about this `restore_processor_state()` call? It's conditional on `CONFIG_KEXEC_JUMP` and `image->preserve_context`. If those are true, we execute that function. Is it KCOV-instrumented? Now I have to check the definition of that function, just to be thorough.

Okay, so `notrace` is just a way to exclude a function from being traced, essentially equivalent to `__attribute__((__no_instrument_function__))` for ftrace. However, that doesn't necessarily imply it's excluded from `__no_sanitize_coverage` unless we're dealing with `noinstr`.  I was looking at `restore_processor_state` in `arch/x86/power/cpu.c`, which is marked `notrace`.

Is `arch/x86/power/cpu.c` compiled with KCOV enabled?  I checked the `arch/x86/power/Makefile` and there's no global disabling of `KCOV_INSTRUMENT` for that directory. The wider `arch/x86` directory doesn't have a global disable either, so KCOV is indeed active in that architecture.

Aha! The commit message is key.  `load_segments()` modifies segment registers, which invalidates the GS base that KCOV relies on for per-CPU data. Subsequent instrumented code calls, such as `native_gdt_invalidate()`, start an endless kernel loop when `CONFIG_KCOV` is enabled.

Right, `restore_processor_state()` in `arch/x86/power/cpu.c` is only called if `image->preserve_context` is true, which requires `CONFIG_KEXEC_JUMP`.  Now, is the `machine_kexec()`'s call to `relocate_kernel_ptr()` instrumented? The indirect function call itself doesn't trigger KCOV, but compiler-inserted instrumentation is the concern. Actually, `machine_kexec()` won't have KCOV instrumentation because `KCOV_INSTRUMENT_machine_kexec_64.o := n` which excludes the whole file.

But what about `__ftrace_enabled_restore(save_ftrace_enabled)`?  It's inline, but could still call other functions internally. It's called at the *end* of `machine_kexec()`. If it's *not* inline, it *will* be instrumented. It *is* inline, from `include/linux/ftrace.h`, but might still call other functions, or just write to a variable.

The main issue is that `physaddr.o` had to be excluded because `__phys_addr()` is called inside `machine_kexec()` via `virt_to_phys(control_page)`. `virt_to_phys()` is an argument to `relocate_kernel_ptr()`. Since `virt_to_phys()` is called *after* `load_segments()`, and it's inline but calls `__phys_addr()`, then `__phys_addr()` was executed *after* `load_segments()`, which caused the crash! If `physaddr.o` is excluded, then `__phys_addr()` is no longer instrumented.

But what if `virt_to_phys()` calls `__phys_addr_symbol()`? No, it calls `__phys_addr()`.  Are there *any* other functions called after `load_segments()` in `machine_kexec()`?

```c
#ifdef CONFIG_KEXEC_JUMP
if (image->preserve_context)
    restore_processor_state();
#endif

__ftrace_enabled_restore(save_ftrace_enabled);
```

If `CONFIG_KEXEC_JUMP` *is* enabled and `image->preserve_context` is true, `restore_processor_state()` *will* be called.  And, importantly, KCOV isn't disabled for `arch/x86/power/cpu.c`!

    "thought": "**Kexec with Preserve Context: A KCOV-Induced Crash**

Okay, so I've been digging into this kexec issue, and here's what I've got. I'm looking at `__ftrace_enabled_restore`, which simply toggles `ftrace_enabled`. Nothing exciting there. Then, I re-examined `arch/x86/power/cpu.c` and specifically `restore_processor_state()`. The `notrace` attribute is there, but wait... `notrace` doesn't protect against KCOV instrumentation, it's `__no_sanitize_coverage` that does the trick via the `noinstr` macro.

Now, `restore_processor_state()` is exported for `apm.c` *and* called from `machine_kexec_64.c`. If KCOV is active, it *will* instrument `restore_processor_state()`, injecting a call to `__sanitizer_cov_trace_pc()`. This function reads the `current` task, which is accessed through `%gs:current_task`.

Here's the problem: in `machine_kexec()`, `load_segments()` is called earlier, which *clears* `%gs` to 0. So, when `restore_processor_state()` is later called (and thus `__sanitizer_cov_trace_pc()` is called), `%gs` is still zero, and boom, a general protection fault or a page fault occurs within `__sanitizer_cov_trace_pc()`.

This is *exactly* the same root cause as the `physaddr.o` issue because `virt_to_phys` inlines a call to `__phys_addr()`.  But the commit only excluded `physaddr.o` and not `restore_processor_state()` or the entire `cpu.o`?

The commit message confirms this, stating the issue can be reproduced with a KCOV-instrumented kernel and a kexec jump. The commit reproduces the `physaddr.o` crash because `virt_to_phys` is unconditionally called for `relocate_kernel_ptr`.

However, `restore_processor_state()` is *only* called if `image->preserve_context` is true. This is the case for kexec *jump* or hibernation.  The standard `kexec -e` command doesn't preserve context, so `restore_processor_state()` isn't called, which hides this bug.

Essentially, if someone uses kexec *with* `preserve_context` (e.g., kexec jump), they will trigger the exact same crash within `restore_processor_state()`.

Let's confirm this.  In `machine_kexec()`, if `image->preserve_context` is true, there are these key calls.
  1. `clear_IO_APIC()`
  2. `restore_boot_irq_mode()`
  3. `load_segments()`; which clears %gs
  4. Calls `relocate_kernel_ptr()`
  5. If `preserve_context` is true,  `restore_processor_state()` is called.

The `relocate_kernel_ptr()` function's job is to relocate the new kernel to its final location, and then jump to it.  So, if `preserve_context` is true, the kernel jumps, later returns, and then immediately calls `restore_processor_state()`.

My question now is what's the state of `%gs` when returning from `relocate_kernel_ptr()`?  `relocate_kernel_ptr` calls the assembly function `relocate_kernel` in `arch/x86/kernel/relocate_kernel_64.S`.  I need to check that assembly to see what it does with `%gs` upon return. I suspect it does *nothing* with `%gs`, leaving it at the zero value it had when `load_segments()` was called.  Thus, the crash!

    "thought": "**Analyzing a Kernel Crash During Kexec Jump**

Okay, so I'm looking at this code path for `kexec` and a potential crash. The new kernel returns to `virtual_mapped` after the jump. It seems like the core issue here stems from the order in which registers, particularly `%gs`, are restored after the kernel relocation during a `kexec` jump.

The `callthunks` code explicitly ignores certain addresses, which makes sense for handling relocations during `kexec`. However, that got me thinking about the return path from `relocate_kernel`. When `kexec -j` returns, it's an assembly `RET`, which *should* be fine within an ignored section, but what happens when we reach `machine_kexec()` again? It's now C code returning from a regular function call.

Here's the problem. If `restore_processor_state()` is called, and it's a *direct* call, it triggers call depth tracking. And that's where the Retpoline mitigation comes in; it tracks *all* calls. The compiler (or `objtool`, more likely) inserts code to adjust `__x86_call_depth` at function entry and exit. But the thunk that's inserted to adjust the counter accesses `%gs:__x86_call_depth`, and, critically, `%gs` is still zero because `MSR_GS_BASE` hasn't been restored yet.

Now I see it! This means it WILL crash with `CONFIG_KEXEC_JUMP` enabled! The commit now disables KCOV for `machine_kexec_64.o`. Okay, so they're masking this, not fixing it! The root cause is a fundamental violation: x86-64 C code is being executed with an invalid `%gs` base. The code is calling `restore_processor_state()` before `%gs` is restored.

And it's even worse. The comment even says it's using per-cpu variables, which confirms my suspicion. The author has completely papered over this issue. This breaks things like KASAN and KMSAN. The real fix? Restore `%gs` BEFORE any C code is executed. That means `MSR_GS_BASE` has to be restored in the `relocate_kernel` assembly code.

The commit disables KCOV for `physaddr.o` (which is useful for fuzzing memory management) and for `machine_kexec_64.o`. And it doesn't even fix the underlying bug for `CONFIG_KEXEC_JUMP`.

Here's what I'll propose. By moving `virt_to_phys(control_page)` call *before* `load_segments()`, we would have entirely avoided the KCOV crash in `physaddr.c`. Then, for `kexec -j`, we'll need to restore `MSR_GS_BASE` in the assembly code. It's a simple `wrmsr` instruction using `saved_context->kernelmode_gs_base` to get the value, or `wrgsbase`, if enabled. I'm going to write a very strong Concern to the author. This is a critical architectural flaw and the current fix is a huge band-aid.

    "thought": "**Analyzing Potential Call Depth Tracking Issues**

Okay, so I'm looking at this code and trying to figure out if there's a problem with function calls after `load_segments()`. The core of the issue revolves around `virt_to_phys()`. Normally, when `CONFIG_DEBUG_VIRTUAL` is NOT set, `virt_to_phys()` is inlined as a macro, like `__phys_addr_nodebug(x)`. No problem there. But, if `CONFIG_DEBUG_VIRTUAL` *is* enabled, it becomes an *actual function call*. This is the critical point.

Now, if `virt_to_phys()` *is* a function, that means it triggers call depth tracking and potentially KCOV, which is what we need to avoid.

Ah, but there's a comment here: "load_segments() resets GS to 0. Don't make any function call after here since call depth tracking uses percpu variables to operate (relocate_kernel() is explicitly ignored by call depth tracking)." The comment is the clue here. The comment clearly states that function calls after `load_segments()` are a no-no because they can mess with percpu variables that are used for call depth tracking.

And then, I recall that C, when it evaluates an argument to a function pointer call, it explicitly calls a function!

Looking at the code:

```c
	load_segments();

	/* now call it */
	image->start = relocate_kernel_ptr((unsigned long)image->head,
					   virt_to_phys(control_page),
					   image->start,
					   relocate_kernel_flags);
```

The issue is immediately apparent. `virt_to_phys()` is called *as an argument* to `relocate_kernel_ptr()`, and that happens *after* `load_segments()`. So, if `virt_to_phys()` is indeed a function call (when `CONFIG_DEBUG_VIRTUAL` is set), it violates the explicit rule: "Don't make any function call after here". This would cause a problem with call depth tracking because it's accessing percpu variables after `load_segments()` has reset GS.

I should check the commit history to see when that warning comment was added - it's highly likely it was added after the fact, or it may have been there and this violation has gone unnoticed. I need to trace back from this point to understand the implications better.

    "content": "{"content":"commit 83214a775f33bc9d61c2c284f2ace3f854a4cddb\nAuthor: Kai Huang <kai.huang@intel.com>\nDate:   Mon Sep 1 18:09:25 2025 +0200\n\n    x86/sme: Use percpu boolean to control WBINVD during kexec\n    \n    TL;DR:\n    \n    Prepare to unify how TDX and SME do cache flushing during kexec by\n    making a percpu boolean control whether to do the WBINVD.\n    \n    -- Background --\n    \n    On SME platforms, dirty cacheline aliases with and without encryption\n    bit can coexist, and the CPU can flush them back to memory in random\n    order.  During kexec, the caches must be flushed before jumping to the\n    new kernel otherwise the dirty cachelines could silently corrupt the\n    memory used by the new kernel due to different encryption property.\n    \n    TDX also needs a cache flush during kexec for the same reason.  It would\n    be good to have a generic way to flush the cache instead of scattering\n    checks for each feature all around.\n    \n    When SME is enabled, the kernel basically encrypts all memory including\n    the kernel itself and a simple memory write from the kernel could dirty\n    cachelines.  Currently, the kernel uses WBINVD to flush the cache for\n    SME during kexec in two places:\n    \n    1) the one in stop_this_cpu() for all remote CPUs when the kexec-ing CPU\n       stops them;\n    2) the one in the relocate_kernel() where the kexec-ing CPU jumps to the\n       new kernel.\n    \n    -- Solution --\n    \n    Unlike SME, TDX can only dirty cachelines when it is used (i.e., when\n    SEAMCALLs are performed).  Since there are no more SEAMCALLs after the\n    aforementioned WBINVDs, leverage this for TDX.\n    \n    To unify the approach for SME and TDX, use a percpu boolean to indicate\n    the cache may be in an incoherent state and needs flushing during kexec,\n    and set the boolean for SME.  TDX can then leverage it.\n    \n    While SME could use a global flag (since it's enabled at early boot and\n    enabled on all CPUs), the percpu flag fits TDX better:\n    \n    The percpu flag can be set when a CPU makes a SEAMCALL, and cleared when\n    another WBINVD on the CPU obviates the need for a kexec-time WBINVD.\n    Saving kexec-time WBINVD is valuable, because there is an existing\n    race[*] where kexec could proceed while another CPU is active.  WBINVD\n    could make this race worse, so it's worth skipping it when possible.\n    \n    -- Side effect to SME --\n    \n    Today the first WBINVD in the stop_this_cpu() is performed when SME is\n    *supported* by the platform, and the second WBINVD is done in\n    relocate_kernel() when SME is *activated* by the kernel.  Make things\n    simple by changing to do the second WBINVD when the platform supports\n    SME.  This allows the kernel to simply turn on this percpu boolean when\n    bringing up a CPU by checking whether the platform supports SME.\n    \n    No other functional change intended.\n    \n    [*] The aforementioned race:\n    \n    During kexec native_stop_other_cpus() is called to stop all remote CPUs\n    before jumping to the new kernel.  native_stop_other_cpus() firstly\n    sends normal REBOOT vector IPIs to stop remote CPUs and waits them to\n    stop.  If that times out, it sends NMI to stop the CPUs that are still\n    alive.  The race happens when native_stop_other_cpus() has to send NMIs\n    and could potentially result in the system hang (for more information\n    please see [1]).\n    \n    Signed-off-by: Kai Huang Okay, so I'm looking at this commit from Kai Huang concerning the `machine_kexec` function, and there are a few things that immediately jump out. They're making a point of loading these segments *before* the Global Descriptor Table (GDT) gets messed with, which makes sense to ensure a stable state. The comment added says "Don't make any function call after here" after `load_segments()`. That's the crux of the issue.

Now, my initial thought was, "But `virt_to_phys` is *already* below `load_segments()` in the original code, so what's the big deal?". Then I realized that the concern isn't about the *position* of the call, but *whether it exists*. If `CONFIG_DEBUG_VIRTUAL` is enabled, `virt_to_phys` *is* a function, calling `__phys_addr()`. This makes it a function call *after* `load_segments()`, and that's a problem.

Here's the problem: arguments to functions are evaluated *before* the function call itself. So even though `virt_to_phys` looks like it's called *inside* `relocate_kernel_ptr`, the argument evaluation happens first.  If that evaluation *is* a function, then the *argument* function executes after `load_segments()` which is exactly what the comment prohibits.

My immediate conclusion is that when `CONFIG_DEBUG_VIRTUAL=y` is enabled, if call depth tracking (or KCOV) is enabled, this will cause a crash due to `__phys_addr()`. The commit *does* disable KCOV for `machine_kexec_64.o` and `physaddr.o`, and that *does* fix the KCOV crash.  The problem is, *it does not fix the call depth tracking problem*! Call depth tracking is still going to catch `__phys_addr()`. And the comment about `relocate_kernel()` being ignored by call depth tracking doesn't help because `__phys_addr()` isn't explicitly ignored!

The simplest and most robust solution would be to move the argument evaluation of `virt_to_phys(control_page)` *above* `load_segments()`. That way, the function call is done before any segment register changes occur.  It seems strange that this wasn't done because this would also solve the KCOV problem with `physaddr.o`. Why disable KCOV for `physaddr.o` instead of moving the argument evaluation?

Now, the analysis needs to get more detailed. Aside from `virt_to_phys`, what other function calls might happen after `load_segments()`? I see calls to `relocate_kernel_ptr`. This is a function *pointer* call and function pointer calls are a bit tricky, and GCC's instrumentation makes this an issue. It would seem like KCOV instrumentation or call depth tracking might be inserted before or after the indirect call. If the compiler enables KCOV at any of these points (or before the arguments in the indirect function call), this will also cause a crash. Because even if the inline assembly `load_segments()` doesn't end a C basic block, the call after it might be in a different basic block that has KCOV instrumentation before it. This explains why KCOV has to be disabled for `machine_kexec_64.o` to prevent KCOV crashes when calling `relocate_kernel_ptr` which is assembly.

However, the comment says "relocate_kernel() is explicitly ignored by call depth tracking". The *function* `relocate_kernel()` (the actual assembly routine) is ignored, but that doesn't include the function *call* to it via `relocate_kernel_ptr`. This is where retpoline comes into play, which is a common defense against retbleed. This is another possible reason to disable KCOV and ensure arguments are computed before segment loads.

It will also occur for *any* function call when Call Depth Tracking, KCSAN, or KMSAN are enabled. Why? Because `load_segments()` sets GS to zero, which causes a page-fault on *any* per-cpu access.

So, how can we fix this properly? The core issue is that `load_segments()` invalidates `GS`, breaking any C code that implicitly depends on per-cpu variables. Okay, so I'm looking at `machine_kexec_64.c`, and I've spotted something interesting. `load_segments()` is resetting `GS` to zero. On x86-64, this means that any attempt to use per-cpu variables will fail immediately after this point. The comment in the code explicitly flags this as a problem, since per-cpu variables rely on the `GS` register for access, which is precisely how KCOV operates! KCOV instrumentation uses per-cpu variables within the `__sanitizer_cov_trace_pc()` function to track code coverage, which means it will fail if `GS` is not properly initialized.

Since `load_segments()` is called within `machine_kexec()` and is a static function, disabling KCOV for `machine_kexec_64.o` effectively prevents any KCOV instrumentation from being triggered *after* that point inside the `machine_kexec()` function. So the code disables instrumentation for this file. Makes sense.

Let's look at the critical code: `load_segments()` sets GS to zero, then `relocate_kernel_ptr` is called to relocate the kernel, possibly followed by `restore_processor_state()` if `image->preserve_context` is true. `restore_processor_state()` should re-initialize `GS`. But *during* the call, KCOV instrumentation might trigger... and potentially fail. However, since `machine_kexec_64.c` is not instrumented, the call itself has no KCOV prologue, but `restore_processor_state()` will be instrumented.

Then I see the `relocate_kernel_ptr` call. This actually relocates kernel code copied to the `control_page`. This part of the code is written in assembly (`relocate_kernel_64.S`), so KCOV instrumentation isn't an issue there. But wait, there's a call to `virt_to_phys(control_page)`. Okay, so I'm looking at the `machine_kexec_64.c` code, specifically the function that handles the kexec jump. The call sequence within it is: `load_segments()`, then `relocate_kernel_ptr()`, and finally, conditionally, `restore_processor_state()` and `__ftrace_enabled_restore()`. My initial thought is the placement of `__ftrace_enabled_restore(save_ftrace_enabled)` after `load_segments()`. That seems a bit off, but let's see how things play out.

The crucial point is that if `image->preserve_context` is *false*, `restore_processor_state()` doesn't get called. So, in that scenario, we just return from `relocate_kernel_ptr()`. But hang on – in a *normal* kexec execution, `relocate_kernel_ptr()` *never* returns; it's the gateway to the new kernel! It's the point of no return.

The twist here is `CONFIG_KEXEC_JUMP`. When we jump *back* to the original kernel, `relocate_kernel_ptr()` *does* return, because we're not rebooting. We're re-entering the original kernel's context. That suggests the `relocate_kernel_64.S` assembly routine needs to do *something* to restore a minimal state, or maybe it's `restore_processor_state` that's doing the heavy lifting.

My immediate question is: does `relocate_kernel` restore GS? I need to check the assembly code, specifically `arch/x86/kernel/relocate_kernel_64.S`. That's where I need to look to understand what state gets restored when returning from the kexec jump.

Okay, so I'm looking at `relocate_kernel_64.S`. This is where the magic happens after the initial kernel relocation. The assembly code here is pretty straightforward, but the details are crucial for understanding the whole process, particularly regarding security and context preservation.

First, it sets up the stack by restoring the saved `rsp`, `cr4`, `cr3`, and `cr0` registers. This is the new execution environment. The comments indicate we are now at a `RET` point with the unwind hint. Now, IF `CONFIG_KEXEC_JUMP` is defined, it *does* restore the GDT. This is significant for ensuring a consistent environment after a kexec jump.  

However, what jumps out at me is that the code *doesn't* restore `MSR_GS_BASE`! This is a critical MSR related to the kernel's ability to protect kernel data, particularly if shadow stacks are being used. It is likely assumed that `MSR_GS_BASE` will be correctly set by the next level of execution.

The assembly then returns, setting the re-entry point for the next time, and restores the general purpose registers (including `rbp`, `rbx`, etc.) from the stack. The unwind annotations are important for debug.

The `ret` instruction hands control back to `machine_kexec()`. Crucially, after the return, `machine_kexec()` then has a call to `restore_processor_state()`. The conditional `restore_processor_state()` in `machine_kexec()` suggests that some context preservation may depend on the value of `image->preserve_context`. The implementation of `restore_processor_state()` is what I will need to look at next to understand what processor state is restored, and confirm the absence of restoration of `MSR_GS_BASE`.

    "thought": "**Analyzing the KCOV Crash in `machine_kexec()`**

Okay, so here's what I'm thinking. I'm looking at this crash related to `machine_kexec()` and KCOV, and I'm seeing a potential sequence that's causing problems.

First, I noticed that `restore_processor_state()` in `cpu.c` calls `__restore_processor_state()`.  I checked the KCOV disable flags in the `Makefile` and quickly realized that while `machine_kexec_64.o` and `physaddr.o` are excluded, `cpu.c` IS instrumented. So that means `restore_processor_state()` and `__restore_processor_state()` within `arch/x86/power/cpu.c` will have KCOV instrumentation enabled.

The crucial point is that `machine_kexec()` calls `restore_processor_state()` *after* `relocate_kernel` returns, and at this point, the MSR `GS_BASE` hasn't been restored.  My reasoning is: The assembly in `virtual_mapped` didn't restore `GS_BASE`—it only touched `cr4`, `cr3`, `cr0`, and `gdt`. Therefore, when KCOV's `__sanitizer_cov_trace_pc()` gets called at the start of `restore_processor_state()`, `GS base` will be zero. This is exactly what caused the author's previous crash loop.

I had to double-check that `machine_kexec()` actually *does* call `restore_processor_state()` after returning from `relocate_kernel`. And yes, it does, under the `CONFIG_KEXEC_JUMP` condition.

Then I realized that `virt_to_phys` is also evaluated *before* calling `relocate_kernel_ptr`. And `load_segments()` is a C inline function call, or macro, so I looked into it.

```c
	load_segments();

	/* now call it */
	image->start = relocate_kernel_ptr((unsigned long)image->head,
					   virt_to_phys(control_page),
					   image->start,
					   relocate_kernel_flags);
```

`load_segments()` is a static function in `machine_kexec_64.c`.  The key here is that it sets the `gs` selector, via assembly:

```c
static void load_segments(void)
{
	__asm__ __volatile__ (
		"\tmovl %0,%%ds\n"
		"\tmovl %0,%%es\n"
		"\tmovl %0,%%ss\n"
		"\tmovl %0,%%fs\n"
		"\tmovl %0,%%gs\n"
		: : "a" (__KERNEL_DS) : "memory"
		);
}
```

...and setting `gs` like this on x86_64, zeroes out the `GS_BASE` MSR!  So, after `load_segments()`, `GS_BASE` is zero. Then the arguments for `relocate_kernel_ptr` are evaluated.  If `virt_to_phys()` is a function call, it's called with `GS_BASE = 0`. If `CONFIG_DEBUG_VIRTUAL=y`, `virt_to_phys` becomes `__phys_addr()`. Now, the commit did disable KCOV for `physaddr.o`, because `__phys_addr` is there. But the code does evaluate `virt_to_phys(control_page)` BEFORE `load_segments()`?  And why not evaluate `image->head` and `image->start` BEFORE?

The code does:

```c
	unsigned long head = (unsigned long)image->head;
	unsigned long start = image->start;
	unsigned long paddr = virt_to_phys(control_page);

	load_segments();
	image->start = relocate_kernel_ptr(head, paddr, start, relocate_kernel_flags);
```

I see.  The assignment `image->start = ...` occurs *after* `relocate_kernel_ptr` returns. That's a key point.  So, when does `relocate_kernel_ptr` return? Only if `CONFIG_KEXEC_JUMP` is enabled, and it jumps back. When it returns, it returns to `machine_kexec()`, which then assigns to `image->start`.  Then it calls `restore_processor_state()`. Okay, so I've been digging into this kexec jump crash on x86-64, and here's the breakdown of my thinking. Initially, I noticed that `arch/x86/power/Makefile` doesn't disable KCOV, KASAN, KMSAN, or KCSAN for `cpu.o`.  That immediately raised a red flag. Then I traced things through `restore_processor_state()`, seeing that it inlines `__restore_processor_state()` which then calls `wrmsrq(MSR_GS_BASE, ...)`.

The problem is, before `MSR_GS_BASE` gets restored, any C code with instrumentation (specifically, KCOV in this case) accesses per-CPU memory using the GS register. Since KCOV *isn't* disabled for `arch/x86/power/cpu.o`, `restore_processor_state()` will have a KCOV trace at its entry point, meaning it attempts to access `this_cpu_read(current_task)` with `GS` base still set to 0. This is a recipe for a `#PF` (page fault) loop, plain and simple.

Now, I checked where `restore_processor_state()` lives (in `arch/x86/power/cpu.c`) and if it's called from `machine_kexec()`. Looking at the code, it's definitely called via `#ifdef CONFIG_KEXEC_JUMP` when `image->preserve_context` is set, confirming that it's executed when returning from a kexec jump.

So, let's address the main concern: this KCOV crash fix is a band-aid masking a deep violation of the x86-64 C execution environment.  Executing C code without a valid `%gs` base is a serious ABI violation.

I looked at the code and noticed `load_segments()` is called and then other C functions. `virt_to_phys()` might be an inline function or external C function if `CONFIG_DEBUG_VIRTUAL=y` and KMSAN may try to instrument memory accesses, using per-CPU metadata contexts.  Then I focused on the line `image->start = relocate_kernel_ptr(...)`. Even if `relocate_kernel` returns successfully, the `MSR_GS_BASE` still has a value of 0 when writing to `image->start`. That's where the problem is.  `image->start = ...` writes to memory *after* `relocate_kernel_ptr()` returns, meaning there's a memory write with GS=0. KMSAN/KASAN could trigger an access to per-CPU data to log the memory access, and crash.  This happens during an important memory write.

Finally, I confirmed that `kexec -e` with `CONFIG_KEXEC_JUMP` *is* a supported feature.  It's used for kernel transitions.

Okay, so I've been digging into this kexec patch, and here's the deal from my perspective. This thing is a bit of a mess, and the fix they put in is like putting a band-aid on a gushing wound, leaving the underlying problem wide open and creating new issues.

First off, they tried to fix a KCOV crash in `machine_kexec` by disabling KCOV for a few specific files, but the approach is fundamentally flawed, especially for `kexec -j`. When kexec jumps to a new kernel, the assembly code in `relocate_kernel` returns to C code, specifically to `machine_kexec()` and the assembly routine *doesn't* restore the GS base. The call to `restore_processor_state()` in C, at that stage, is the problem, but disabling KCOV for a few source files that include `__phys_addr()`, that doesn't fix it. It just *hides* it. If `CONFIG_KEXEC_JUMP` is enabled (and `image->preserve_context` is true), that's where the code returns after `relocate_kernel` returns, and at that point the GS base is still zero! The following call to `restore_processor_state()` will then trigger the KCOV instrumentation at the *start* of the function. Meaning it will crash the kernel. The whole point of the patch was to solve the crash, but it completely misses this use case, leaving it broken and still vulnerable, creating an endless loop of page faults. To fix this, they *have* to restore `MSR_GS_BASE` *before* returning to the C code, in assembly, or pre-evaluate the variables so the rest of the code works with a zeroed base.

Now, this problem isn't just about KCOV. This patch also masks a major violation of the x86-64 C execution environment. The code resets the GS base to zero using `load_segments()`, but then it continues to evaluate C code and access memory, assuming everything is fine. The code tries to make a C call *after* `load_segments()`, specifically, `virt_to_phys(control_page)`. If `CONFIG_DEBUG_VIRTUAL` is enabled, this becomes `__phys_addr()`, a C function. This is a big problem. The compiler has a comment about this, and it *will* crash the system if there are any other instrumentations that rely on GS like call-depth tracking. Also, it also involves memory reads/writes. Sanitizers like KMSAN and KCSAN that look at memory accesses also get involved here. Reading `image->head` and `image->start` and writing to `image->start` *after* `load_segments()` with the zeroed GS base, these memory accesses, they will crash too. The fix is not to disable KCOV, but to do the evaluations, the memory reads and writes *before* `load_segments()` to avoid the problem entirely.

And then there's the coverage loss. To avoid the KCOV crash, the patch disables KCOV for `physaddr.o`. This includes core physical address translation functions like `__phys_addr()`. Now, by disabling KCOV globally for this file, they're *reducing* KCOV coverage. This has a direct impact on the effectiveness of tools such as syzkaller, and reduces coverage of some very important code. It's totally unnecessary. If the evaluations were moved *before* `load_segments()`, the `__phys_addr()` calls would run with a valid GS base and coverage could be maintained. The fix should not come at the cost of global coverage.

So, in short, this fix is incomplete, it masks the underlying problem, and it creates *new* problems. The correct fix is to address the underlying architectural issue in `relocate_kernel`, fix the function arguments to be evaluated first, and *not* disable KCOV.

    "content": "commit 5519152dd8ec28f6a05a8c4d002224d4a6a140e3
Author: Aleksandr Nogikh <nogikh@google.com>

x86/kexec: Disable KCOV instrumentation after load_segments()

This commit disables KCOV instrumentation for machine_kexec_64.o and
physaddr.o to prevent crashes during kexec. The crashes occur because
load_segments() invalidates the GS base, which KCOV uses to access
per-CPU data, leading to a crash loop in subsequent instrumented code.

> diff --git a/arch/x86/kernel/Makefile b/arch/x86/kernel/Makefile
> index e9aeeeafad17..febf6f49207b 100644
> --- a/arch/x86/kernel/Makefile
> +++ b/arch/x86/kernel/Makefile
[ ... ]
> @@ -43,6 +43,17 @@ KCOV_INSTRUMENT_dumpstack_$(BITS).o			:= n
>  KCOV_INSTRUMENT_unwind_orc.o				:= n
>  KCOV_INSTRUMENT_unwind_frame.o				:= n
>  KCOV_INSTRUMENT_unwind_guess.o				:= n
> +# Disable KCOV to prevent crashes during kexec: load_segments() invalidates
> +# the GS base, which KCOV relies on for per-CPU data.
> +# As KCOV && KEXEC compatibility should be preserved (e.g. syzkaller is
> +# using it to collect crash dumps during kernel fuzzing), disabling
> +# KCOV for KEXEC kernels is not an option. Selectively disabling KCOV
> +# instrumentation for individual affected functions can be fragile, while
> +# adding more checks to KCOV would slow it down.
> +# As a compromise solution, disable KCOV instrumentation for the whole
> +# source code file. If its coverage is ever needed, other approaches
> +# should be considered.
> +KCOV_INSTRUMENT_machine_kexec_64.o			:= n

Is the CONFIG_KEXEC_JUMP path still affected by the crash loop this tries to
fix?

When relocate_kernel() returns, execution resumes in machine_kexec():

	/* now call it */
	image->start = relocate_kernel_ptr(...);

#ifdef CONFIG_KEXEC_JUMP
	if (image->preserve_context)
		restore_processor_state();
#endif

Since restore_processor_state() is located in arch/x86/power/cpu.c, and that
file is not excluded from KCOV instrumentation, would the compiler insert a
call to __sanitizer_cov_trace_pc() at the start of the function?

If so, this would trigger per-CPU accesses with a zeroed GS base before the
function has a chance to restore MSR_GS_BASE, leading to the exact same crash
loop.

Would it be better to address this architecturally, such as restoring
MSR_GS_BASE natively in the assembly return path of relocate_kernel()?

> diff --git a/arch/x86/mm/Makefile b/arch/x86/mm/Makefile
> index 5b9908f13dcf..3a5364853eab 100644
> --- a/arch/x86/mm/Makefile
> +++ b/arch/x86/mm/Makefile
[ ... ]
> @@ -4,6 +4,8 @@ KCOV_INSTRUMENT_tlb.o			:= n

x86/kexec: Disable KCOV instrumentation after load_segments()

This commit disables KCOV instrumentation for machine_kexec_64.o and
physaddr.o to prevent crashes during kexec. The crashes occur because
load_segments() invalidates the GS base, which KCOV uses to access
per-CPU data, leading to a crash loop in subsequent instrumented code.
